defs: A tier, row, or rank. See Tier.
quotes: In posture to displode their second tire Of thunder.

records.ety: [Aphetic form of attire; OE. tir, a tir. See Attire.]

defs: A covering for the head; a headdress.
quotes: On her head she wore a tire of gold.

defs: A child's apron, covering the breast and having no sleeves; a pinafore; a tier.
A ring, hoop or band, as of rubber or metal, on the circumference of the wheel of a vehicle, to impart strength and receive the wear. In Britain, spelled tyre.
notes: ☞ The iron tire of a wagon wheel or cart wheel binds the fellies together. The tire of a locomotive or railroad-car wheel is a heavy hoop of iron or steel shrunk tightly upon an iron central part. The wheel of a bicycle or road vehicle (automobile, motorcyle, truck) has a tire of rubber, which is typically hollow inside and inflated with air to lessen the shocks from bumps on uneven roads.

defs: To adorn; to attire; to dress.
quotes: [Jezebel] painted her face, and tired her head.

records.ety: [F. tirer to draw or pull; of Teutonic origin, and akin to E. tear to rend. See Tirade.]

defs: To seize, pull, and tear prey, as a hawk does.
quotes: Even as an empty eagle, sharp by fast, Tires with her beak on feathers, flesh, and bone.
Ye dregs of baseness, vultures among men, That tire upon the hearts of generous spirits.

defs: To seize, rend, or tear something as prey; to be fixed upon, or engaged with, anything.
quotes: Thus made she her remove, And left wrath tiring on her son.
Upon that were my thoughts tiring.

records.ety: [OE. teorien to become weary, to fail, AS. teorian to be tired, be weary, to tire, exhaust; perhaps akin to E. tear to rend, the intermediate sense being, perhaps, to wear out; or cf. E. tarry.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Tired (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Tiring.]

defs: To become weary; to be fatigued; to have the strength fail; to have the patience exhausted; as, a feeble person soon tires.
To exhaust the strength of, as by toil or labor; to exhaust the patience of; to wear out (one's interest, attention, or the like); to weary; to fatigue; to jade.
quotes: Tired with toil, all hopes of safety past.
